next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

By the time Anika got off the Q66 and crossed Northern Boulevard, she had already done the math three different ways and every version made her chest tight.

If she stayed at Zoe's apartment until nine, she would miss one hundred and forty minutes of footwork, one full ghosting block, and forty serves from the left box. If she left at eight-thirty, she could still get in fifty minutes in the hallway between the bedrooms, if Dad was out on app-driving and did not hear the ball taps. If she left at eight, she could do all of it except the serves.

Then she remembered she had promised Zoe she would stay for cake, and cake was never at eight.

The plastic gift bag handle sawed a red line into her fingers. Inside was a sketchbook with thick paper, the best one she could afford at the pharmacy after school, plus a six-pack of neon pens that clicked when she shook the bag. The cashier had offered a glitter card for another three dollars. Anika had said no and watched the woman put it back on the spinning wire rack without looking at her.

Zoe's building smelled like laundry steam and old frying oil. On the second-floor landing, the number two had fallen off one apartment door and somebody had drawn it back with black marker, a crooked swan neck of ink. Music thudded through the wall before Anika knocked: bass from a pop song, a burst of shrieking laughter, then the flat digital gunfire of a video game.

Zoe opened the door on the second knock, already grinning, chipped black nail polish flashing as she grabbed Anika's wrist and hauled her in.

"You made it. Holy shit,...
open_hooks_to_carry: Fee-waiver appointment requires income and debt disclosure.
Anika sees itemized oncology debt total for the first time.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...e enough for questions. She chose time.

In the apartment, the kitchen light was still on. Ravi had moved from table to couch, one arm over his face. The ice pack sat melted in the sink.

Anika showered with one foot out of the spray because hot water made the shin throb harder. In her room she opened Maya's notebook to the current week page. She had drawn columns after chapter three and kept them strict: date, session, load, pain (0-10), notes.

Monday AM — ghosting/lunge matrix/feeds/suicides — pain 7 rising to 8 on right tibia.

Monday PM — modified tech session — pain 6 with spikes.

Late PM — unauthorized suicides — pain 8, near buckle, visible swelling.

She stared at the numbers until they blurred. Then she uncapped her pen and dragged a line through each one, slow and careful so the page would not tear.

Monday AM pain 4.

Monday PM pain 3.

Late PM mobility only.

In the notes column she replaced near buckle with managed with form correction.

The edits looked almost neat, as if there had never been other numbers under them. She took the nurse note from the notebook pocket, unfolded it, and read NO RUNNING/JUMPING UNTIL CLEARED one more time. Then she folded it into a rectangle small as a tea tag and tucked it inside the back cover where the binding had come loose.

From the living room came Ravi's sleep cough, dry and stubborn. Anika closed the notebook, pressed her palm flat over it, and listened until the apartment settled into pipes and distant traffic and the soft electrical buzz of the refrigerator.

Her shin kept pulsing anyway, steady as a second heartbeat.
state_deltas_required: Submit hardship paperwork for tournament fees.
Anika converts grief into a rescue narrative: winning becomes her imagined route to fixing family debt, escalating self-imposed pressure.
